query: predictive capability of fecal calprotectin for relapse risk in inflammatory bowel disease

retrieved_docs.title: Inflammatory Bowel Disease -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis for both UC and CD depends on the extent of the disease and treatment response. The stool markers lactoferrin and calprotectin are useful in determining the postoperative recurrence of CD. Some evidence exists that these can also be used to predict future flares. However, patients with IBD tend to have much higher mortality compared to the general population. Causes of death include primary disease, infections, and respiratory illness. Heart disease is not a risk factor for death in IBD. Finally, psychological morbidity is very high in IBD patients, and the quality of life is poor.

retrieved_docs.title: Crohn Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Treatment Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: A fecal calprotectin of less than 50 μg/g is sufficient to rule out disease recurrence in patients who have remained asymptomatic 12 months after surgical remission. Routine endoscopic assessment is not required in these patients. The same biomarker and target may be used in asymptomatic individuals with low postoperative recurrence risk or higher postoperative recurrence risk but already on prophylactic medication. High-risk patients not on prophylactic medication should undergo endoscopic evaluation for disease activity assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: Crohn Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Treatment Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Suggested biomarkers are fecal calprotectin and serum CRP. Targets should be less than 150 μg/g for calprotectin and less than 5 mg/L for CRP to rule out active inflammation and avoid routine endoscopic evaluation of disease activity. The AGA further recommended the following: Endoscopic or radiologic confirmation of disease remission within 3 years of symptomatic remission Biomarkers every 2 to 4 months to assess therapeutic response during active disease A follow-up endoscopy should be performed 6 to 12 months after symptom resolution to document mucosal healing.

retrieved_docs.title: Pseudoappendicitis -- Evaluation -- Calprotectin
retrieved_docs.content: Calprotectin may be of benefit in the diagnosis of pseudoappendicitis. Calprotectin is secreted into the intestinal lumen during the early stages of intestinal mucosal damage. Notably, calprotectin demonstrates resistance to bacterial degradation at room temperature, enhancing its stability as a biomarker. The collection of samples for calprotectin is noninvasive. Fecal calprotectin has already shown its value as a diagnostic marker for various bowel pathologies, including chronic inflammatory bowel diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: Crohn Disease -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: High-risk patients with moderate-to-severe Crohn disease often have the following characteristics: Relatively younger age, ie, younger than 30 years History of active or recent tobacco consumption Elevated CRP or fecal calprotectin levels Deep ulcers on colonoscopy Involvement of long bowel segments Perianal disease Extraintestinal manifestations History of bowel resections [56] [57]

retrieved_docs.title: Inflammatory Bowel Disease -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Continued surveillance for dysplasia is critical for long-standing patients with UC. The cumulative risk of colorectal cancer is estimated to be as high as 30% for those with the disease of 30 years or more. The extraintestinal manifestation of primary sclerosing cholangitis leads to liver failure. [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Crohn Disease -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Without immunomodulator or biologic therapy, up to 50% of patients may become steroid-dependent or treatment-resistant, with a cumulative incidence of abdominal surgery of 46.6% 10 years after diagnosis. [58] The postsurgical endoscopic recurrence risk at or above the surgical anastomosis is around 90%. Risk factors for early recurrence include cigarette smoking, short duration between Crohn disease diagnosis to the first surgery, need for multiple resections, and penetrating disease. [59] Surveillance endoscopy is recommended in 6 to 12 months once a patient has had surgery. Ileocolonoscopy should be repeated in 1 to 3 years without endoscopic recurrence.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Diarrhea -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Fecal leukocytes/calprotectin/lactoferrin are markers of inflammation.  The presence of these markers will point towards inflammatory diarrhea, such as Crohn disease, or ulcerative colitis.

retrieved_docs.title: Crohn Disease -- Evaluation -- Miscellaneous Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Baseline thiopurine methyltransferase (TPMT) levels should be checked before deciding on treatment options. Low TPMT levels may increase the risk of side effects, while very high levels may decrease the effectiveness of prescribed treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Mesalamine (USAN) -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify appropriate indications for mesalamine therapy in inflammatory bowel disease, including ulcerative colitis and potential off-label uses in Crohn disease after surgery. Implement evidence-based dosing strategies for mesalamine based on disease severity and patient-specific factors. Select appropriate adjunctive therapies or alternative treatment options when mesalamine monotherapy is inadequate or contraindicated. Coordinate follow-up care and monitoring for patients on long-term mesalamine therapy, emphasizing the importance of medication adherence and regular disease assessment. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Inflammatory Bowel Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Stepwise Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: The immune-modifying agents (eg anti-TNF agents) are the step three drugs. These are used when the patient does not respond to corticosteroids, steroids are required for prolonged periods, or the steroids cannot be tapered down without recurrence of symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: Collagenous and Lymphocytic Colitis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Many patients with microscopic colitis have a chronic, intermittent course of disease symptoms. Diarrhea can sometimes resolve without treatment or often resolve with treatment within weeks. Relapses of symptomatology are frequent. Currently, it is unclear if 1 form is worse than the other. At present, there is no associated increased risk of colorectal cancer with microscopic colitis.

retrieved_docs.title: Extraintestinal Manifestations of Inflammatory Bowel Disease -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: IBD has a waxing and waning course, and most patients maintain an active, healthy lifestyle. Treatment and lifestyle modifications help manage the symptoms of the disease. Careful screening and early management of EIMs improve health, quality of life and reduce morbidity. EIMs associated with intestinal-IBD disease activity manifest during flare-ups of the disease, whereas EIMs independent of intestinal-IBD severity may precede IBD diagnosis or occur during IBD remission.

retrieved_docs.title: Inflammatory Bowel Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Stepwise Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: A stepwise approach that is used for IBD management is outlined below.

retrieved_docs.title: Crohn Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Treatment Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: The AGA recommended in 2023 that monitoring Crohn disease treatment response should be based on the presence of clinical symptoms and biomarker levels. [50] Biomarkers may be taken every 6 to 12 months in patients in remission and more frequently in those requiring therapy titration.

retrieved_docs.title: Carcinoembryonic Antigen -- Clinical Significance -- Carcinoembryonic Antigen Measurement in Colorectal Cancer Diagnosis and Posttreatment Surveillance
retrieved_docs.content: The sensitivity and specificity of serial CEA measurement in detecting recurrent colorectal cancer are approximately 80% and 70%, respectively. CEA monitoring has the advantage of providing a lead time of about 5 months compared to other diagnostic methods for detecting recurrence. Early detection and timely resection increase the chance of surgical cure when treating recurrent tumors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1673
retrieved_docs.content: Vankomycin and fidaxomicin are both recognized therapeutic agents for the management of التهاب معوي قولوني غشائي كاذب (pseudomembranous colitis). These antibiotics are employed to eradicate the underlying Clostridioides difficile infection that characterizes this condition, thereby reducing inflammation, preventing recurrence, and improving patient outcomes. Effective use of either vankomycin or fidaxomicin is an important component of evidence‑based treatment strategies for pseudomembranous colitis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1524
retrieved_docs.content: Fidaxomicin is an oral macrocyclic antibiotic that is specifically indicated for the treatment of التهاب معوي قولوني غشائي كاذب (pseudomembranous colitis), a severe infection of the colon caused primarily by Clostridioides difficile. By targeting C. difficile while sparing much of the normal intestinal microbiota, fidaxomicin helps eradicate the pathogen and reduce recurrence rates compared with some other agents. Its use is especially valuable in patients with moderate to severe disease or in those who have not responded adequately to first‑line therapies, contributing to improved clinical outcomes and more effective management of this potentially life‑threatening colonic inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 143
retrieved_docs.content: chronic inflammatory small bowel disease and in secondary diabetes mellitus with diabetic chronic kidney disease, underscoring the importance of recognizing a chronic clinical course across diverse organ systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2629
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic inflammatory small bowel disease is characterized by a chronic clinical course, indicating a prolonged and persistent disease trajectory. This chronic pattern of disease progression is similarly observed in secondary diabetes mellitus that is complicated by diabetic chronic kidney disease, where the condition also follows a chronic clinical course. The shared chronic nature of these distinct disorders underscores the importance of recognizing that patients with chronic inflammatory small bowel disease may be susceptible to metabolic disturbances such as secondary diabetes mellitus and its renal complications, necessitating vigilant monitoring and integrated management of both gastrointestinal and endocrine‑renal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2260
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic inflammatory small bowel disease is characterized by a chronic clinical course, indicating that the disease persists over time and often follows a prolonged, relapsing pattern. This chronic clinical course is also typical of secondary diabetes mellitus that occurs in the setting of diabetic chronic kidney disease, as well as of chronic kidney disease itself (قصور كلوي مزمن). The shared chronic trajectory among these conditions underscores the interconnection between persistent intestinal inflammation, secondary forms of diabetes, and progressive renal impairment. Consequently, patients with chronic inflammatory small bowel disease should be closely monitored for the development of secondary diabetes and for early signs of diabetic kidney disease, while individuals with secondary diabetes mellitus and chronic kidney disease require vigilant management of both glycemic control and renal function to mitigate further disease progression.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1659
retrieved_docs.content: Aminosalicylic acid and similar agents are classified as members of both the mesalamin (mesalamine) group and the sulfasalazine group, reflecting their role as 5‑aminosalicylic acid derivatives that are incorporated into these related therapeutic classes used in the management of inflammatory bowel disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 901
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic inflammatory small bowel disease is characterized by a chronic clinical course. This chronic course can evolve into secondary diabetes mellitus with diabetic chronic kidney disease and also into chronic kidney failure (قصور كلوي مزمن). In addition, glomerulonephritis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى) is clinically associated with chronic kidney failure (قصور كلوي مزمن). These interconnections indicate that patients with chronic inflammatory small bowel disease are at risk for metabolic complications such as secondary diabetes and subsequent renal impairment, and that glomerulonephritis may further contribute to chronic kidney dysfunction. Recognizing and monitoring these linked conditions are essential for comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 351
retrieved_docs.content: The Medicament patch test product is derived from bacitracin, indicating that bacitracin serves as its parent compound. Both bacitracin and fidaxomicin are linked to the management of pseudomembranous colitis, a severe inflammatory condition of the colon, suggesting that they may be considered in therapeutic strategies for this disease. However, bacitracin, desmopressin, and the tromethamine salt of ketorolac each have a contraindication in the presence of renal disorder, meaning that patients with impaired kidney function should avoid these agents. Recognizing the potential renal risks associated with bacitracin, desmopressin, and tromethamine‑ketorolac, while also understanding their possible roles in treating pseudomembranous colitis, is essential for safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 544
retrieved_docs.content: Aminosalicylic acid and similar agents are classified as members of the mesalamin group and function as intestinal antiinflammatory agents. Antiallergic intestinal antiinflammatory drugs, excluding corticosteroids, are also categorized as intestinal antiinflammatory agents. Additionally, corticosteroids acting locally are recognized as intestinal antiinflammatory agents. Together, these drug classes—mesalamin derivatives, antiallergic non‑corticosteroid agents, and locally acting corticosteroids—share the therapeutic role of reducing inflammation within the gastrointestinal tract, highlighting the diverse pharmacologic approaches available for managing intestinal inflammatory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1661
retrieved_docs.content: Aminosalicylic acid and similar agents are classified as members of the mesalamin group, indicating that they belong to the same therapeutic class.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2575
retrieved_docs.content: Diverticulosis and diverticulitis are classified as رَتْجٌ مِعَوِيّ, indicating that both conditions fall under this specific disease category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2269
retrieved_docs.content: Aminosalicylic acid and similar agents are classified as members of the sulfasalazine compound, indicating that these 5‑aminosalicylic acid derivatives constitute the active moiety released from sulfasalazine after colonic bacterial metabolism. Sulfasalazine, a combination of sulfapyridine and 5‑aminosalicylic acid, is employed in the treatment of ulcerative colitis and Crohn disease, and its therapeutic effect relies on the release of aminosalicylic acid and related agents within the intestine. Recognizing aminosalicylic acid and its analogues as integral components of sulfasalazine helps clinicians understand the drug’s mechanism of action and potential side‑effect profile.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2022
retrieved_docs.content: Exploratory laparotomy and ثقب معوي (intestinal perforation) are frequently observed together in clinical practice. An exploratory laparotomy is often performed when a ثقب معوي is suspected or identified, as the surgical approach allows direct visualization and management of the perforated bowel. Recognizing the co‑occurrence of these conditions is important for timely diagnosis and appropriate operative intervention, which can reduce morbidity and improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 969
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic inflammatory small bowel disease is characterized by a chronic clinical course, a pattern that is also observed in secondary diabetes mellitus with diabetic chronic kidney disease and in chronic kidney failure (قصور كلوي مزمن). Necrotizing glomerulonephritis represents a specific, severe form of glomerulonephritis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى) and is closely linked to anti‑basement membrane disease (داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي), an autoimmune condition that manifests as renal dysfunction (اختلال وظيفة الكلى). In addition, necrotizing glomerulonephritis is frequently associated with pulmonary‑renal syndrome (متلازمة رئوية كلوية), reflecting its systemic impact. Glomerulonephritis, in turn, is clinically associated with the development of chronic kidney failure, underscoring the progressive nature of renal injury in these disorders. Together, these relationships highlight how chronic inflammatory processes, autoimmune kidney diseases, and metabolic complications interrelate and contribute to long‑term renal impairment, informing comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 175
retrieved_docs.content: diverticulosis and diverticulitis are grouped within the “unspecified functional gastrointestinal disorder” SMQ (الاختلال الوظيفي غير المحدد في الجهاز الهضمي SMQ), a category that also includes reflux esophagitis, GERD, dyspepsia and diarrhea, highlighting shared functional relationships among upper and lower gastrointestinal disturbances. This SMQ grouping underscores the similarity of pathophysiological uncertainty among these disorders, facilitates a unified diagnostic and therapeutic approach, and supports more effective patient‑care decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1982
retrieved_docs.content: Constipation (إِمْساك) is clinically associated with vomiting (تَقَيُّء), indicating that patients who experience difficulty passing stool may also present with episodes of emesis, reflecting a possible shared gastrointestinal dysfunction or underlying condition that links these two symptoms.
